items.myth: Hierarchical clustering always finds the true number of clusters automatically.
items.reality: The algorithm builds a full tree; you still choose where to cut it (or how many clusters to keep). The dendrogram informs that choice but does not replace judgment or validation metrics.
items.example: A dendrogram may show a plausible 3-cluster cut and an equally plausible 5-cluster cut two heights apart—silhouette scores or domain needs decide which to use.

items.myth: Single linkage and complete linkage always give similar clusters.
items.reality: Single linkage uses the minimum cross-cluster distance (prone to chaining); complete uses the maximum (favors compact blobs). The same data can produce radically different trees.
items.example: Two elongated Gaussian blobs bridged by a few points may merge early under single linkage but stay separate longer under complete linkage.

items.myth: A dendrogram height directly equals Euclidean distance between cluster centroids.
items.reality: Merge height records the linkage-defined dissimilarity at merge time, which depends on the linkage rule and may not equal centroid distance except in special cases (e.g. Ward with Euclidean).
items.example: Under average linkage, merge height is the mean pairwise distance, not ||μ_A - μ_B||₂ between centroids μ_A and μ_B.
